exemplar.title: I did Computer Science and Psychology World studies EE.

detailedExemplarData.title: I did Computer Science and Psychology World studies EE.

strengths: The topic and research question are communicated clearly and situated effectively within an interdisciplinary lens
Methods are linked to IB syllabus concepts (e.g., humanâcomputer interaction, sociocultural approach), demonstrating informed selection
The introduction and table of contents outline a coherent plan for exploring both technological and psychological dimensions
weaknesses: The scope remains rather broad, occasionally diluting analytical focus across multiple constructs
Key definitions (e.g., digital competence) are sometimes truncated or repeated, reducing conceptual precision
Methodological details (sampling criteria, interview protocols) are insufficiently specified, limiting transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates a wellâcommunicated topic and research question with generally appropriate, interdisciplinary methods. However, the breadth of focus and lack of methodological detail keep it within the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects strong communication and informed method selection, without the full narrow focus or comprehensive justification required for higher marks.

strengths: Source selection includes a wide range of peer-reviewed articles, government reports, and context-specific Korean studies, showing expert relevance
Disciplinary terminology (e.g., self-efficacy, ergonomics, sociocultural approach) is used accurately and consistently throughout
Visual data and case-study materials are directly integrated to support conceptual explanations
weaknesses: A minority of citations lack full publication details or use tertiary web sources for foundational definitions
Some sections remain descriptive, with limited synthesis of contrasting perspectives
Occasional informal phrasing (âIn other wordsâ¦â) detracts slightly from academic tone
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs knowledge demonstration is coherent, well integrated, and underpinned by expertly chosen, highly relevant sources, meriting a top-band score. Minor citation and stylistic lapses prevent the absolute top mark of 6, but overall the work exhibits depth and consistency that justify a score of 5.

strengths: Research is grounded in triangulated sourcesânational statistics, focus-group interviews, peer-reviewed studiesâdemonstrating rigorous selection and contextual relevance
Analysis links empirical findings to core concepts (e.g., accessibility barriers, digital-skill deficits) and generates practical recommendations
The argument follows a clear interdisciplinary structure, acknowledging limitations and drawing coherent conclusions
weaknesses: Analysis occasionally recounts data descriptively rather than interrogating causality or alternative explanations
Critical evaluation of counterarguments (e.g., efficiency benefits of kiosks) is only briefly addressed
Some methodological limitations (small FGI sample, broken URLs) are noted but not deeply interrogated in the argument
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work exhibits sustained critical thinking: research is mostly rigorous and directly relevant, analysis is coherent, and the argument is structured with interdisciplinary insight. Although there are lapses in depth of causal analysis and counterargument evaluation, the overall critical engagement justifies a high-band score of 10 out of 12.

strengths: Clear academic structure with coherent headings and logical progression through interdisciplinary sections
Well-integrated visuals (Venn diagrams, survey graphs) that are captioned and referred to in the text
Consistent title page, table of contents, page numbering, and overall formatting reinforce readability
weaknesses: Figure numbering is inconsistent and some images are low resolution or contain untranslated text
The bibliography mixes citation styles and includes incomplete entries, with occasional missing publication details
A few tables (e.g., Figure 6) are densely packed and would benefit from clearer labels and layout
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation is generally effective: the essay adheres to academic conventions, employs relevant visuals, and is well structured. Minor lapses in figure numbering, visual quality, and referencing consistency prevent a completely flawless execution. A score of 4 (top of the 3â4 band) reflects this strong but imperfect presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay follows a logical progressionâdefinitions, technological lens, psychological lens, results, and conclusionâcreating a discernible argument that kiosks exacerbate digital exclusion but can be mitigated through competence-building. Cross-references between CS and psychology provide interdisciplinary linkage, and limitations are acknowledged in the final section.  

Critical evaluation appears in discussing sample size, self-report bias, and effectiveness of training methods, indicating some insight. Yet the depth of comparative evaluation between disciplines remains moderate, and counter-arguments (e.g., benefits of kiosks for efficiency) are only briefly noted.  

Consequently, the argument is âstructured with some critical insightâ but lacks the sophistication and nuanced evaluation required for the top band, warranting a score of 6 within the 4â6 range.

The student employs footnote citations linked to a final bibliography formatted broadly in a consistent style, and page numbers, title, and word count are present. Most sources are identifiable, and in-text superscripts correspond to bibliography entries, demonstrating understanding of academic conventions.  

However, formatting of references lacks uniformity; several entries are missing key details (e.g., year, publisher, full URLs), and some hyperlinks are broken or incomplete. In-text citations are occasionally absent when claims are made, and the bibliography mixes styles (APA-like with web citations).  

These issues place the work in the 1â2 band for this strand, but evidence of systematic referencing justifies the upper mark of 2. (Score 3 is not available in this band, hence 2 is awarded.)

The essay presents a title page with word count, an automated table of contents, clearly numbered sections, and sub-headings that mirror the interdisciplinary structure. Paragraphing is logical and transitions are generally smooth, enabling reader navigation.  

There are, however, occasional formatting inconsistencies (e.g., duplicate figure numbers, misaligned tables) and some excessively long sections that could be subdivided for readability. These lapses prevent full adherence to best academic practice.  

Overall the structure is recognisably academic and coherent, fitting the 3â4 band; a mid-band score of 3 reflects solid but not exemplary execution.

The essay demonstrates a solid grasp of key ideas such as digital competence, accessibility, ergonomics, self-efficacy, and social conformity, and applies them to the South-Korean kiosk context. Explanations of digital-competence components and their link to IB syllabus areas show understanding.  

Application of knowledge, however, is sometimes descriptive; large sections reiterate literature without fully synthesizing implications or interrogating opposing perspectives. The primary data are interpreted, yet depth of linkage between CS and psychology theories could be stronger.  

Overall, knowledge is clear and generally well used, meeting the descriptors for 3â4, with a mark of 4 awarded for consistent, if not fully integrated, application across sections.

The student analyses survey tables, focus-group transcripts, and literature to draw conclusions about technological and psychological barriers. Links between empirical findings and concepts such as accessibility, digital skills, and social pressure are generally clear, and the discussion leads to practical recommendations (e.g., prototype practice kiosk).  

However, much of the âanalysisâ recounts data rather than critically interrogating causality or alternative explanations. Conclusions are plausible but occasionally asserted rather than fully demonstrated with nuanced reasoning. Some leaps (e.g., from small FGI findings to national policy recommendations) are insufficiently qualified.  

The analysis is therefore âcoherent with largely supported conclusions,â aligning with the top of the 4â6 band; a mark of 6 is justified.

The research questionââWhat is the relationship between digital competence and the development of kiosks that affect the daily life of senior citizens in South Korea?ââis stated explicitly in the opening page and revisited in the conclusion. It does direct the investigation toward both technological and psychological effects, showing a purposeful interdisciplinary intent.  

Nonetheless, the wording bundles several variables (digital competence, kiosk development, and daily life impacts) without specifying the measurable parameters or scope (e.g., particular aspects of daily life or specific indicators of competence). This breadth occasionally leads to descriptive rather than tightly focused analysis, indicating partial, but not full, focus.  

Consequently, the question meets the descriptors for being âclear but only partially focused,â meriting the upper mark of the 3â4 band.

The majority of sources are policy reports, peer-reviewed journal articles, and primary data gathered by the student; they directly relate to senior citizens, kiosk technology, and digital competence, showing deliberate relevance. National statistics and domain-specific Korean research strengthen contextual authenticity.  

Source triangulation between quantitative surveys, qualitative FGIs, and international literature reflects thoughtful selection. Nevertheless, occasional peripheral or generic web resources dilute overall rigour, and the sample size for primary data is small.  

Given that most research material is well chosen and clearly pertinent but not uniformly rigorous, the work fits the lower end of the 7â9 band; a score of 7 is appropriate.

The methodology section explains an interdisciplinary design combining focus-group interviews with seniors and secondary statistical/academic sources from computer-science and psychology. The student links each data-gathering technique to curriculum concepts such as âHumanâcomputer interactionâ and the âSociocultural approach,â demonstrating awareness of disciplinary tools.  

Justification is presentâe.g., primary FGI to obtain lived experiences; secondary Korean surveys for quantitative breadthâbut the rationale remains mainly descriptive. Details about sampling, interview protocols, and data analysis procedures (beyond a brief mention of Creswellâs spiral) are limited, and potential methodological limitations appear only in the conclusion.  

Given the generally appropriate yet somewhat surface-level explanation, the work fits the 3â4 band, earning the top mark of 4 for showing âsome evidence of informed selection,â though not the completeness and critical justification expected at 5â6.

The bibliography lists a wide range of academic journal articles, government reports, and industry sources, many of them in Korean, which are highly relevant to the local focus. Visual data such as the NIA Digital Information Gap Survey and Jeongâs training study directly support the argument.  

Nonetheless, some citations are incomplete (broken URLs, missing publication details), and there is reliance on tertiary webpages (e.g., IB CompSci Hub) for conceptual definitions better sourced from peer-reviewed literature. The selection is therefore âmostly relevant and appropriateâ rather than exemplary.  

These characteristics align with the 3â4 band, and the higher mark of 4 is awarded because most core evidence is clearly pertinent and drawn from credible academic studies.

Numerous figures (graphs, tables, Venn diagrams, screenshots) are integrated, captioned, and referred to in-text. Images support explanation of digital competence and survey data, enhancing communication.  

Nevertheless, some visuals are low-resolution, contain untranslated Korean text, or repeat information; a few captions are duplicated, and figure numbering is inconsistent. The studentâs own prototype screenshots, while relevant, crowd the appendix and are not critically analysed in-text.  

Given generally effective but imperfect use and labelling, the work meets the descriptors for 3â4, with a score of 3 indicating minor but noticeable weaknesses.

The student employs disciplinary terms such as âdigital skills,â âergonomics,â âself-efficacy,â âsocial pressure,â and âsociocultural approachâ with reasonable accuracy, signalling comfort with both computer-science and psychological lexicons. Definitions are provided for core constructs, aiding clarity.  

Some terminology is occasionally applied in a generic or repetitive manner, and a few conceptual distinctions (e.g., between âdigital skillsâ and âknowledgeâ) blur in later analysis. Minor language inaccuracies and informal phrasing appear, suggesting that precision is solid but not consistently sophisticated.  

Thus, terminology usage is âmostly accurateâ and conceptually informed, meriting a score of 4 within the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The title clearly states the investigationâs focus and includes the World Studies lens, and the word count is correctly provided, meeting formal IB formatting requirements.

The research question bundles digital competence, kiosk development, and daily life impacts without specifying measurable parameters or narrowing scope; consider defining key variables or focus areas more precisely.
Citing âIB CompSci Hubâ as a primary source diminishes scholarly rigor; replace or supplement with peerâreviewed literature for foundational claims.
Accessibility guidelines are outlined without adapting them to the specific needs or context of Korean senior users; contextualize recommendations with local examples.
While you propose an intuitive interface example, integrating FGI quotes or data here would ground recommendations in participant experiences.
Including a study on teachersâ AI attitudes (Figure 5) feels tangential; clarify its direct relevance to senior kiosk use or replace with more pertinent data.
The table of contents maps out a logical structure and interdisciplinary flow, guiding the reader through technological and psychological analyses effectively.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent and missing publication details (e.g., publisher location); ensure all references follow a uniform style.
Linking each method to specific IB curriculum units (e.g., Humanâcomputer interaction and Sociocultural Approach) demonstrates informed methodology selection and focus.
Methodology lacks detail on sampling procedures and interview protocol, limiting transparency and replicability; include recruitment criteria and interview guides.
Figure 3âs Venn diagram succinctly illustrates how digital skills, knowledge, and attitudes intersect to form digital competence, aiding conceptual understanding.
Indentation levels in the contents are inconsistent, making the hierarchy of subsections unclear; adjust formatting for consistent visual structure.
In the introduction, statements about the causes of digital exclusion are asserted without direct empirical support; link each claim to specific evidence or literature.
The definition of digital competence is truncated mid-sentence, undermining conceptual clarity; ensure complete, wellâintegrated definitions.
The text relies heavily on secondary definitions without critically evaluating source authority or context; incorporate source criticism for conceptual depth.
Figure 6âs table is densely packed and not wellâlabelled; improve visual clarity by simplifying layout and adding clear row/column headings.
The sociocultural approach section links theory well but misses opportunities to tie these constructs back to FGI or survey insights for deeper analysis.
Survey data in Figure 7 remain purely descriptive; consider adding basic statistics (e.g., mean, median) or visual summaries to support analytical depth.
The sample size, recruitment criteria, and context for the fastâfood survey are not described; include methodological details to strengthen validity.
The caseâstudy table on page 14 aggregates many participants with missing demographic detail; specify selection rationale and ensure consistency across entries.
Some phrasing remains informal (e.g., âIn other wordsâ¦â); refine language to maintain a consistently academic tone and precise terminology.
Discussion of digital skills is abstract; link each highlighted skill directly to challenges faced by South Korean seniors using kiosks.
Explicitly tying digital skills discussion to IBâs âHuman interaction with the systemâ unit strengthens the interdisciplinary focus and methodological coherence.
Figure 5âs pyramid model is introduced but not sufficiently explained in the text; elaborate on how each level applies to digital competence development.
Figure 5 lacks details on sample size and demographics for the teacher study, reducing transparency; provide participant information to bolster credibility.
